tweets.full_text:
Claude Codeエンジニアさん曰く、
「AIによる文章や資料の出力を、MarkdownではなくHTMLで作らせるほうが便利」
らしい

・HTMLのほうが表現力が高い
→表、見た目調整、図解、コード例、インタラクションなどを、HTMLやCSSでまとめて表現できる
・見た目整うから共有しやすい
・生成速度が2〜4倍

でもMarkdownに比べて文字数めちゃくちゃ増えるからトークン効率悪そう、あくまで人に見せるものはHTMLで管理〜くらいがいいんかな？

ずっと悩んでいた。

アイデアはある。でも、やり切れない。
手が足りない。時間が足りない。

Claude Codeを使い始めて、それが変わった。
「脳みそが広がった」という感覚を、初めて理解した。

アイデアをやり切る仕組みが手に入ると、支援できる質が変わる。

聞いていいですか？

あなたが「やりたいのに、まだできていないこと」は何ですか？

quoted_status.full_text:
Claude CodeでCodexを呼び出すやつ、やっぱ神設定すぎたわww

Claud Codeが出力したアウトプットをCodexが毎回自動で確認する設定とかができたり、Claude Codeの最大の弱点である画像生成機能をつけることができる。

しかもCodexはサブエージェントとして動くから、Claude Codeのコンテキストやトークン消費が増えるとかもほぼほぼないのがマジで強いし、やばいです。  

Claude Codeをメインで使いつつ、Codexの得意領域はCodexのサブエージェントを呼び出してタスクを任せる。 Claude Code or Codex問題、これで解決じゃね？？  

需要多ければワイのフルAI事業にどんな感じでCodex組み込んでるか今度まとめたやつ出すかもです！

tweets.full_text:
Claude Codeはプログラミングもすごいけど、これ1つで非IT仕事を任せられるのがデカいよね。
・会計処理
freee MCPで会計ソフトfreeeを操作。
管理画面を開くことなく、指示するだけで経費の登録や決算処理の大半が終わる。
・スライド資料
イベントで喋る内容や学習教材で使うスライド資料を作成。

@corbin_braun @cursor_ai Non-tech guy who started with Cursor, now primarily on Claude Code. Once you go terminal-first, the IDE starts feeling like overhead. If Cursor 3 is heading there too, they've read the room.

user.name: Adel Bucetta

tweets.full_text:
Something I'm noticing with Claude Code/Codex is that the productivity gain is highly uneven, and almost entirely depends on how clear your product vision is going in. "I want to build something like XYZ..." mostly produces slop. But if you have a concrete plan and execute it component by component, the throughput is genuinely ~10x. The model is doing the typing, you're doing the architecture.

The weirdest part is that it's addictive. Normally, writing software is slow meaning you have to debug, wait, search, fix, and repeat. With these tools, the gap between "I want this" and "it works" shrinks to minutes. Your brain starts treating it the way it treats games, with constant small wins, and you keep going.

tweets.full_text:
35 MCP SERVERS YOU SHOULD HAVE INSTALLED YESTERDAY 

claude code without mcp is just a chatbot.

with mcp it controls every app you use.

1. filesystem - let claude read and edit any file on your mac
2. github - full repo access, push, pr, review, merge
3. gitlab - same as github but for gitlab projects
4. brave-search - real web search inside your prompt
5. fetch - pull any url and parse it on the fly
6. postgres - query your prod db with plain english
7. sqlite - same for any sqlite file
8. supabase - db, auth, storage, edge functions, all of it
9. slack - read channels, post messages, react, archive
10. gmail - read inbox, draft replies, label, send
11. google-drive - search, read, edit any doc or sheet
12. notion - read pages, query databases, create entries
13. linear - create issues, move tickets, leave comments
14. jira - same for jira boards
15. sentry - pull stack traces, mark resolved, get context
16. stripe - query customers, refund, lookup invoices
17. shopify - read store, edit products, fulfill orders
18. hubspot - pull crm contacts, log calls, update deals
19. airtable - query bases, edit rows, run formulas
20. figma - read design files, export frames as images
21. perplexity - deep research without leaving the terminal
22. e2b - claude writes code and runs it in a real sandbox
23. puppeteer - full chrome control with one command
24. playwright - better puppeteer with mobile emulation
25. cloudflare - manage workers, dns, kv, r2, all of it
26. memory - persistent memory across all your sessions
27. sequential-thinking - claude thinks step by step out loud
28. obsidian - your entire vault becomes claude context
29. apple-notes - read and search your notes app
30. apple-reminders - claude manages your todos
31. spotify - control playback, search, build playlists
32. youtube - search videos, pull transcripts, summarize
33. discord - read servers, dm, post in channels
34. telegram - read chats and groups, send messages
35. time - claude finally knows what time it actually is

HOW to install any of these in 10 seconds:

run this in your terminal → claude mcp add <name> npx <package>

example → claude mcp add github npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-github

paste your api token if it asks. done.

full install commands for all 35 → https://t.co/Ua3TVAS2Z8

once you install 5 of these your terminal becomes the only app you need.

i was paying $40 a month for raycast, $20 for grammarly, $15 for a research tool.

Claude code does all of it now for FREE

↓ save this so you can install these in this weekend

user.name: Raemond
user.description: Tweets: electronics hacking, cycling, and local politics. Work: Building consumer electronics. Kouign amman connoisseur

quoted_status.full_text:
LLM Knowledge Bases

Something I'm finding very useful recently: using LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for various topics of research interest. In this way, a large fraction of my recent token throughput is going less into manipulating code, and more into manipulating knowledge (stored as markdown and images). The latest LLMs are quite good at it. So:

Data ingest:
I index source documents (articles, papers, repos, datasets, images, etc.) into a raw/ directory, then I use an LLM to incrementally "compile" a wiki, which is just a collection of .md files in a directory structure. The wiki includes summaries of all the data in raw/, backlinks, and then it categorizes data into concepts, writes articles for them, and links them all. To convert web articles into .md files I like to use the Obsidian Web Clipper extension, and then I also use a hotkey to download all the related images to local so that my LLM can easily reference them.

IDE:
I use Obsidian as the IDE "frontend" where I can view the raw data, the the compiled wiki, and the derived visualizations. Important to note that the LLM writes and maintains all of the data of the wiki, I rarely touch it directly. I've played with a few Obsidian plugins to render and view data in other ways (e.g. Marp for slides).

Q&A:
Where things get interesting is that once your wiki is big enough (e.g. mine on some recent research is ~100 articles and ~400K words), you can ask your LLM agent all kinds of complex questions against the wiki, and it will go off, research the answers, etc. I thought I had to reach for fancy RAG, but the LLM has been pretty good about auto-maintaining index files and brief summaries of all the documents and it reads all the important related data fairly easily at this ~small scale.

Output:
Instead of getting answers in text/terminal, I like to have it render markdown files for me, or slide shows (Marp format), or matplotlib images, all of which I then view again in Obsidian. You can imagine many other visual output formats depending on the query. Often, I end up "filing" the outputs back into the wiki to enhance it for further queries. So my own explorations and queries always "add up" in the knowledge base.

Linting:
I've run some LLM "health checks" over the wiki to e.g. find inconsistent data, impute missing data (with web searchers), find interesting connections for new article candidates, etc., to incrementally clean up the wiki and enhance its overall data integrity. The LLMs are quite good at suggesting further questions to ask and look into.

Extra tools:
I find myself developing additional tools to process the data, e.g. I vibe coded a small and naive search engine over the wiki, which I both use directly (in a web ui), but more often I want to hand it off to an LLM via CLI as a tool for larger queries. 

Further explorations:
As the repo grows, the natural desire is to also think about synthetic data generation + finetuning to have your LLM "know" the data in its weights instead of just context windows.

TLDR: raw data from a given number of sources is collected, then compiled by an LLM into a .md wiki, then operated on by various CLIs by the LLM to do Q&A and to incrementally enhance the wiki, and all of it viewable in Obsidian. You rarely ever write or edit the wiki manually, it's the domain of the LLM. I think there is room here for an incredible new product instead of a hacky collection of scripts.
